title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Introduction
content: Men with low-grade tumors rarely die from prostate cancer. They are much more likely to die from other causes first. On the other hand, men with high-grade disease are more likely to die from prostate cancer within ten years without radical treatment. The prognosis of men with intermediate-risk cancer is the most difficult to predict. The perceived increased risk of mortality compared to low-risk disease can often push clinicians towards radical treatment in these cases. Still, such a strategy has been challenged by the findings of studies such as the ProtecT study of treatments for localized prostate cancer. Prognostic markers and scoring will likely be of great interest to this group of patients. [8]

title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 242
content: The knowledge base describes prostate cancer as a malignant tumor of the prostate (prostata) that may extend directly into the seminal vesicles (semenné váčky), representing a specific manifestation of cancer of the prostate. Malignant prostate tumors are grouped in the SMQ (أورام البروستاتا الخبيثة) and include metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي) as well as other prostatic disorders (اضطراب بروستاتي), which are classified under other male genital disorders and are linked to screening (التحصّي). Therapeutic management is organized under Operations by approach, with open prostatectomy classified as an inverse‑isa member of this category; open prostatectomy is a prostate operation (عمليات البروستاتا) that excises prostate tissue and may partially excise the seminal vesicles, and it is associated with the SMQ for treatment of malignant tumors. Additional therapeutic male genital procedures include prostatolithotomy, prostatotomy, calculus removal, and transurethral prostate incision (شق البروستاتا عبر الإحليل), all defined as child procedures within the “other or therapeutic procedures; male genital” group. Diagnostic evaluation is captured by diagnostic procedures; male genital, with transrectal biopsy of the prostate as a child entity that partially removes prostate tissue and is linked to prostate operations. Together, these entities and relationships provide a comprehensive framework for understanding prostate‑related malignancies, their extension to adjacent structures, and the spectrum of diagnostic and therapeutic interventions.

title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 52
content: Malignant tumor involving the seminal vesicle by direct extension from the prostate represents a specific manifestation of prostate cancer that spreads into the seminal vesicles (semenné váčky). Prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا) is located in the prostate (prostata) and belongs to the group of malignant prostate tumors (أورام البروستاتا الخبيثة (SMQ)). The prostate is the anatomic site for metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي), prostate disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي), and other male genital disorders, which are also classified under اضطراب بروستاتي. Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions frequently co‑occur with a range of prostate‑related diseases, including prostate disorder, bladder stone (حصاة حالبية), prostate cancer, metastatic prostate cancer, and benign prostatic hyperplasia (فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد, تضخم بروستاتي). Clinically, transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) is associated with both benign prostatic hyperplasia and prostate cancer, reflecting its therapeutic role in relieving obstruction caused by prostatic enlargement and in managing significant prostate pathology. Surgical management of prostate disease is grouped under prostate operations (عمليات البروستاتا). Open prostatectomy, an operation classified by approach, involves excising the prostate (excised_anatomy_has_procedure) and may partially remove the seminal vesicles (partially_excised_anatomy_may_have_procedure); it targets malignant prostate tumors among its operative indications. Transrectal biopsy of the prostate is another procedure within the broader category of prostate operations. These relationships illustrate how prostate cancer and its extensions, associated genitourinary conditions, and the various diagnostic and therapeutic procedures—including TURP, open prostatectomy, and transrectal biopsy—are interlinked within the anatomy of the prostate and seminal vesicles, guiding comprehensive patient assessment and management.
